As such, the market is very influenced by the changing world around it. For instance, Russia saw its real estate market dominated by “ Krus c hevka ”-style buildings after the war, due to a great increase in demand and need for  cheap  housing in cities. Coming back to 2023, the trend of sustainability and overall environmental consciousness has seen a large increase in popularity, and has led to the creation of various ways of ensuring companies work in an ethical way and take care of the environment.  For instance, the ESG score helps investors decide which companies follow ethical guidelines and “address risks related to Environmental, Social and Governmental issues in its day-to-day operations” . Other certifications like B-Corp can help consumers understand  which companies have a strong commitment to transparency and a high social & environmental performance . Finally, specifically for properties, the WELL certification ensures that the house or building is “designed to support the health and wellbeing of their occupants” .   Therefore,  as these trends grow, so does the need for more sustainable buildings. According to the EEBA ( Energy&Environmental  Building Alliance), as much as 41% of global energy consumption is caused by building s and structures . Hence, making buildings more sustainable is truly a priority and will make a huge impact on the environment as well as help reduce the maintenance costs in the long run.   Hence, t he final outcome of the project will be to create a scenario-based strategy using the Deloitte scenario planning framework  for Kiinteistömaailma with 4 scenarios that have 5 dimensions which will in turn create 4 strategies that the company will be able to pursue. The object of the paper is Kiinteistömaailma (later  in the paper  referred to as KM), which is a real estate company based in Finland which operates in the Baltics region. The subject of the paper is KM’s scenario-based strategy. PART I: Analysis  o f the company in the  F innish market Part 1.1  General overview of the company Part 1.1.1 Introduction to Kiinteistömaailma Kiinteistömaailma is one of the biggest Real Estate companies in Finland. Founded in 1990 by  Postipankki  bank (currently called Danske Bank), Sampo insurance company and West Invest, the firm focuses on selling and renting apartments, houses and corporate offices in the Baltics region. The turnover of the company was 6.84 million euros and the result for the financial year was 670,000eur. Turnover increased by 7.9% if compared to the previous year. The operating profit percentage was 29.9%.  The form of the company is Limited Company (OY -  osakeyhtiö  in Finnish). It is currently the 3 rd  largest real estate firm in Finland in terms of available property for sale. Moreover, the company has over 600 real estate agents in 100 offices spread over the country. The firm has been an early precursor in the industry, when they introduced a new way of selling apartments with a bidding system called “ Tarjouskauppa ” (Offer sale). In this new technique, the listed property price is a starting price, and the customers can make offers upwards. The seller can then choose to accept or reject offers that come to him, and buyers can see the offer history to make a higher offer. After its introduction in 2007, over 30,000 properties were sold using this method.  The goal of this method is to create competition between buyers, and make the price as close to the market one as possible. Kiinteistömaailma sells many different types of property, ranging from small apartments (<100,000eur) to big houses (>1,000,000eur).  Their business model is focused around brokerage. In essence, the goal of a broker is to connect a buyer and a seller together through various marketing techniques and methods.  Buyers are interested in acquiring various types of property (houses, apartments) for a price that is as low as possible and sellers are willing to sell their property for the highest possible price.   Beyond finding the customers, a broker’s job is also to successfully manage the property they are selling through the visits and open houses and by taking flattering pictures that value the property in question. Once a buyer and a seller have agreed on a sales price, the realtor prepares the necessary documentation and makes sure that the funds from the buyer are successfully transferred. In the process, the broker receives a commission from the final sales price (usually between 3%-4% in Finland).  The main way of marketing properties is through their online portal ( www.km.fi  – the 3 rd  largest in Finland), where all properties that are on sale are listed on the website. Another way of obtaining customers is through direct mailing (email, physical post letters) and calling.  Kiinteistömaailma doesn’t have a lot of suppliers but they are essential to the growth of the company. For instance, in order to have a working website, the firms  need  a hosting company. For Kiinteistömaailma, the supplier is Amazon Web Services (AWS).  Moreover, in order to increase brand awareness and obtain more customers, KM needs to use social media platforms like Instagram and Facebook, both of which are owned by Meta.  Agencies located in Helsinki According to CBRE Research , the Finnish real estate market is very attractive to investors due to low political instabilities and good overall environment for investing. The country quickly recovered from the crisis caused by Covid with a growth of 20% in real estate investment volumes from 2020 as compared to 2021. The volumes reached 7,14bln  eur  in 2021. Looking at private property real estate, Finland is also extremely competitive and attractive. Looking at the figure below, it is possible to see that cross-border investment volume (foreign investment) has gone from almost nonexistent to around 30-40% of total investments. Moreover, the overall residential investment volume has gone from a couple millions to several billion, with the current number being 2,4bln EUR invested.  Residential investment volume and cross border share To conclude, Kiinteistömaailma is a Finnish real estate company that operates in a thriving residential market. Part 1.1.2  Overview of the industry and   e xternal environment analysis Kiinteistömaailma competes on the real estate market, a market that has been steadily growing. Since there are more and more people being born, and big cities keep growing, there is high demand but limited space, which in consequence rises the prices of real estate property. The COVID crisis just made things even better, with a lot of people deciding to buy additional real estate to spend time away from the city during the pandemic. It is possible to see that the market had a size of 65bln EUR in 2017, and as mentioned previously, that the share of international investors keeps growing (Fig. 3). The next graph below (Fig. 4) also shows that the amount of property sold has increased in the recent years, which shows that the market is quite dynamic. There was a dip in sales in 2020 due to the pandemic, and then a large increase, where around 10,000 more property was sold in 2021 than in 2020. The market has stabilized in the beginning of 2022, while still having more sales than in 2019 for instance. As the price per m2 is growing in the whole country, so does the amount of sales (as people are willing to make profit on their houses and more demand = higher price), meaning that the real estate company will have an increasing amount of sales to perform, which is a positive result for them. Source: Official Statistics of Finland, apartment prices Now, moving to the main competitors of Kiinteistömaailma, there are several names that come into light. Real Estate agencies like Re/max, Huoneistokeskus. OP-Koti and Bo are the ones that compete with the firm, as they also sell property all around Finland. However, out of all these firms, Kiinteistömaailma is still the biggest in Finland both in terms of apartment on sale and in number of offices in the country. In terms of services proposed, it is pretty much the same for all of these agencies. The only notable difference between each agency is the commission taken by the real estate agents themselves, which ranges from 1,5 to 5% of the final sales price, depending on the price of the property. To some extent, each individual Kiinteistömaailma’s agencies also compete between each other. Hence, the firm is the largest real estate agency in Finland, which is their main competitive advantage. Most agents working in the firm have over 20 years of experience, and know precisely how to set a price to sell the apartment fast. Moreover, with the use of  Tarjouskauppa  (bidding sale), the market price is always set. All in all,  Kiinteistömaailma  have the image of a real estate agency for families. Their goal is to sell a dream, with a focus on long term living (not really investment). With a simple green branding, the firm is recognizable in all of Finland.  Finally, moving to the external environment analysis of the Real Estate agency. There are several factors affecting the company, more than in many other industries. Starting with political factors. Since prices of property are closely tied to the level of inflation, each change in price ( e.g.,  rise in gas prices) will consequently affect the price of real estate. A raise in price of gas means a decrease in consumer welfare and therefore a rise in prices of materials, hence increasing the overall cost of housing. Moreover, since land is scarce and is the main reason for the level of real estate prices, a change in the laws regarding ownership of land in the city could potentially destroy the real estate market. If the city of Helsinki suddenly decides to sell all of the land that it owns, there would be a large amount of land available, thus reducing the prices of real estate. Laws regarding social housing could also affect the company’s business. If the government decides to allocate more houses for people will small revenues, then there will be less property available for Kiinteistömaailma to sell. Many Economic factors affect the business. Welfare of people, mortgage rates and inflation level, are all influencing the price of real estate, and more importantly the size of the commission. Before the Covid crisis hit the world, commissions for sale in Finland were around 4-5% of the sales price. Now, commissions are between 1,5-2.5% for apartments, and maximum 3% for houses. This is due to a higher level of competition between real estate agencies. Unemployment levels also strongly affect the real estate market. According to IZA, the German Institute of Labor Economics, unemployment levels are strongly negatively correlated to real estate prices, meaning that a higher level of unemployment means a lower price level of property. Talking about Social factors, one essential factor to talk about is Demographics. Some areas in Helsinki have more old than young people, and some have more foreigners than locals. All of these factors influence the price of real estate positively and negatively. Hence, Real Estate agents must be aware of these changes and be ready to adapt. In terms of technology, most sales are now performed using the DIAS-system (Digital Apartment Sale), which means that the buyers and sellers don’t have to leave their home to sign the ownership contract. Moreover, only a few portion of clients still come to the agency to seek for property. Most inquiries are done by email, phone, directly on the company’s website, or using a sales portal, which regroups all property sales in one website. Part 1.2 The global market   & the sustainable  real  estate  market Part 1.2.1 The real estate market The real estate business has been growing worldwide for  decades and is a constant source of profitability for investors.  With a market size of over $3,5 trillion in 2021  and an expected CAGR of 5,2% between 2022 and 2030, it is one of the largest industries in the world and considered as the 3 rd  biggest industry worldwide in terms of revenue . The main attractiveness of the industry comes from its stability, short term returns and low barrier to entry . Any property owner may actually be considered an investor, since the value of their property keeps increasing over time.  The overall rate of return, accessibility and guaranteed returns makes this industry attractive for investors. Moreover, housing is always needed and could be considered a basic human need. In contrast, this is not the case for the stock market which isn’t a necessity and therefore holds a higher risk of collapse. Part 1.2.2 Sustainability in real estate A new  trend that has been impacting the markets in a positive way is the arrival of sustainable buildings.  Since defining sustainability is quite complex, so is the definition of a sustainable building (also called green building). Simply put, “a green building is an environmentally sustainable building, designed, constructed and operated to minimize the total environmental impacts” .  Such projects have emerged due to the increasing concern of sustainability from consumers and investors. A study by McKinsey in 2023  has shown that consumers and investors are truly interested in buying and investing into projects and firms that have a social concern (such as sustainability).  Consumers were ready to pay more for a sustainable product than one which had no regard in the matter. Looking at the market of green buildings, the value of the market was at $550 billion in 2021 and is expected to have a CAGR of 12% between 2022-2030 , which is over 2 times more than the traditional real estate market. This phenomenon can be  explained  by the  paper from  Nidomulo  et al., 2009 about how  sustainability has become the key driver of innovation  by (1) companies transforming the compliance to regulations into sustainable innovation, (2) encouraging companies to make their supply chains more efficient and (3) them seeing value in becoming more sustainable by seeing that consumers are ready to pay the surplus for sustainability. Beyond consumers, major shareholders are also interested in the shift towards sustainability.  In his paper, Hart, S, 2003 , argues that “a sustainable enterprise is one that contributes to sustainable development by delivering simultaneously  economic , social and environmental benefits –  the so -called triple bottom line”. In essence, a sustainable firm can create benefits for all of its stakeholders and the environment in which they exist, which is the reason why such firms are more attractive for shareholders. Summary of sustainable value framework strategies applicable to real estate industry Now that we know what value sustainable buildings bring to the market, we can analyze in a more precise manner the specificities of the industry and how the business model has changed. In order to back up our theories, we will use the  following  book: “Drawdown:  The Most Comprehensive Plan Ever Proposed to Reverse Global Warming ”  by Paul Hawken. Part 1.2.3 New technologies in real estate W e can look at the specific technological advancements in the green real estate industry based on the work of Paul Hawken and his book  and systemize the processes according to the value chain.  Net-Zero Buildings Such building is called net-zero because it has zero net consumption, meaning it produces as much energy as it uses in a year. The time frame is important, because some periods of the year are more energy-hungry (such as cold winters), while other seasons require less energy consumption (summer). Such buildings are possible due to the technological advancements made in recent years and due to small incremental changes done in each field.  There are many ways of reducing energy loads in buildings. For instance, electrical lighting can be ditched in favor of natural sunlight wherever possible.  Floors can be designed in a way to mitigate the need for elevators and encourage walking. Insulation can be improved to achieve maximum heat retention in the winter and maintain coolness in summer. Windows can be made electrochromic which will change the opacity according to weather conditions (windows become darker in summer). Finally, the air conditioning can be designed in a way to mimic natural ventilation principles from nature, such as thermite mounds , which can help to reduce energy usage and increase efficiency. On a more human side,  All of these changes, once implemented, help to make each building more efficient and reduce the environmental impact. According to Hawken, if 10% of  new  buildings were net-zero by 2050, the integrated opportunity would be 7.1 gigatons of carbon dioxide.  In comparison , the whole European continent  emitted around 2,7gigatons in 2021 , meaning that the impact of green buildings is huge. Green roofs Traditional roofs on buildings don’t serve much purpose beyond the protection from rain and moisture. They can heat up to 90 degrees C higher than the air in summer and thus transfer the heat to the rest of the building, increasing the need for use of the AC. In contrast, green roofs create veritable ecosystems in the sky and use the forces of natural ecosystems to reduce the carbon emissions of buildings.  By having plants on top of buildings, it can help reduce the overheating problem. Moreover, the whole roof can be transformed to be used for community purposes as well – such as garden, park or even barbecue space where people can spend time together. Example of well implemented green roof Such roofs have benefits on all 3 levels of the Triple Bottom Line approach. They help reducing the Greenhouse Gases emissions (GHG) by limiting the need for cooling in summer and the heating in winter (environmental  aspect ). They increase the help of inhabitants by making summers easier to go through and by  incentivizing  social exchanges on roofs . This will result in greater well-being caused by natural beauty   which is the result of biophilia (humanity’s innate affinity to the natural world)  (social aspect) . Finally, this initiative also reduces costs, since there will be less need for AC usage and increased rent price for landlords, since these roofs add value to the real estate (financial aspect). If green roofs represented 30% of all roof space by 2050, it would reduce CO2 emissions by 0.8 gigatons. LED Lighting By replacing traditional, inefficient lightbulbs with LED’s, a reduction of about 70% of energy consumption could be seen. Moreover, with the increase in the price for electricity, the higher price of the lightbulb itself can be compensated thanks to lower prices in the long run. Assuming that 90% of lightbulbs will be LED  by  2050, an estimated 7,8 gigatons of CO2 can be avoided for residential buildings and an additional 5 gigatons for commercial buildings, making this option the most  effective in reducing pollution caused by buildings. Heat pumps   A heat pump is a great alternative for heating in the winter and cooling in the summer . It works by compressing the cold air from outside thus making it warm. It also works the other way around, and can pull out hot air from inside and send it out. Functioning of a heat pump If the use of a heat pump is combined with the use of renewable energy, the energy consumption is  very low and the need for fuel consumption is reduced to zero. The emissions reductions would amount to 5.2 gigatons of CO2, in the case where heat pumps replace traditional heating methods. Smart thermostats Although technology keeps getting better and better, most homes are still equipped with old-fashioned ways of controlling temperature at home. They require manual operation or preset programming, which most people are not very reliable at using. One of Google’s recent  projects  is Nest, which is a smart thermostat that is capable of changing the temperature of a room by using your phone. Moreover, it even learns from your own habits and adapts the temperature based on what you enjoy most. For instance, it reduces the heating while you are not at home or during sleep  which helps to save energy. According to independent studies, it reduces the costs of energy by around 15% . Beyond helping the environment by using less energy, it also helps the inhabitants by reducing their spending on energy bills. If 46% of households that have internet will start using smart thermostats by 2050, it could avoid 2.6 gigatons of CO2 emissions. Insulation One of the most  cost-effective  solutions that can be implemented is improved insulation. By managing to better retain heat in winter, buildings will reduce the need  for constant use of radiators and heaters. It also helps to improve the quality of the air and keeping out moisture. Moreover, the insulation does not need a lot of maintenance, so its life cycle is very long, resulting in assured return on investment. If 54% of current buildings installed efficient insulation, 8.3 gigatons of emissions could be avoided. Retrofitting The idea of retrofitting is to implement the steps mentioned above in older buildings. For instance, replacing traditional gas-fueled heaters by heat pumps, improving the insulation and replacing traditional lightbulbs with  LEDs  in older houses can lead to a huge increase in quality of life. It will lead to a decrease in energy usage and improved health, hence helping both the social and environmental aspects. On average, 159  tons  of CO2 are saved per retrofitting project .  Water distribution According to the World Bank, around 8.6 trillion gallons (32,5 trillion liters) of water is wasted every year because of malfunctioning piping and leaks in those pipes. This in turn means that municipalities have a sinking bottom line. Moreover, poor regions of the world have sometimes  up  to 50% of their pipes in bad condition, which further causes damages on the communities.  Hence, improving water distribution efficiency by managing the pressure inside of the pipes and controlling leakages can help to address climate change and reduce quadrillions of liters of water over the next 30 years. Completed on October 28, 2022, it is one of the most innovative green buildings worldwide  and uses most of the new technologies mentioned by Paul Hawken is his book Drawdown which was analyzed  in the previous part . The green building located on 425 Park Avenue, New York City, NY, USA.  By introducing various technologies, the building is not only  uses  lower amounts of energy compared to other buildings, but also has systems which makes the building “healthy”. Indeed, (1) the AC system installed purifies the air and filters out smoke and CO2 coming from the city. Next, (2) the whole building is equipped with floor-to-ceiling curtains that “support the human circadian rhythms and promotes visual, mental and biological wellness” . Another way in which this building is healthy for its inhabitants is by (3) removing outside noises and creating an ideal acoustic environment, making for a noise-free experience and (4) encouraging movement inside of the building by having many indoor and outdoor spaces for people to walk around in. Finally, the last innovation lies in (5) the piping system, which is created to ensure the cleanliness of the water using sensors that can detect impurities.  All of these technologies create a building that is truly human-oriented and ensure the wellness of workers. According to Awan, A.G et al. , there is a positive correlation between a healthy work environment and productivity, meaning that people who work in a  high-quality  environment are more productive than people who work from a dusty office. In order for people to be able to differentiate between normal and “green” buildings, there are certifications which are created. Such certifications set standards and rules for buildings which then guarantee the consumer that the building does indeed provide health benefits for its occupants. For the Park Avenue building, it is WELL Platinum certified (the maximum level  achievable ) thanks to the 103 criteria points  that set them apart from other non-certified buildings (80 points are required for the Platinum certification). The main outcome from this case is the fact that green buildings are not only developed to benefit the exterior environment (reducing CO2 emissions, reducing GHG emissions) but also to have benefits on the social level (health of workers) which in turn creates additional levels of profit (increased productivity). This coincides with the Triple Bottom Line theory by John  Eklington  in the California Management Review in 1994 , which states that companies should focus on the social and environmental issues as much as on profits.  To conclude, the 425 Park Avenue Case shows us how sustainable buildings can shape the future of living and working by implementing new technologies which improve the living conditions of people while also improving the environment in which we live through the increase of energy efficiency of these buildings. All of these factors contribute to the “People, Planet, Profit” approach which shows that business can be conducted in a way that creates not only financial value, but can also benefit the environment and society as a whole. This will help us understand which external factors affect the industry and thus will help us develop a strategy for the expansion of KM in France.  Political Economic Political stability Governmental grants Inflation Increasing mortgage rates Increasing buying power Social Technological Increase in social consciousness and interest in sustainability Increase in population Unemployment DIAS-system Technological advancements in sustainable building Smart cities Augmented reality Environmental Legal Environmental harm Carbon neutrality & sustainability Summary table of PESTEL analysis of the real estate market in France Political Political stability The real estate industry is heavily dependent on politics and laws, in which governments can manage both supply and demand. Political stability is very important, and countries with low political stability are often underinvested into and neglected. On the other hand, countries like France which are quite stable have less risks related to policies and therefore are more attractive for investors from all around the globe. Governmental grants In order to promote and incentivize investors and real estate project developers to build green buildings, the French government set up a grant of 350M euros in 2022. Beyond the financial help, developers also get access to faster building permits and the government has set the objective of renewing buildings at a pace of 1% of the current number of buildings per year (ex: if a city has 1000 buildings, they expect 10 new ones to be built per year) . The size of the grant depends on the number of apartments that are in each building. For all buildings that have more than 2 apartments, the gran t  represents 1500euros per apartment.  Economic Inflation Since the start of the conflict between Ukraine and Russia, inflations levels have increased worldwide and France has been particularly affected. According to the Central Bank of France, average inflation levels were at around 5,9% in 2022, and are expected to be as high as 7% by the end of 2023 .  This will affect the number of people who are able to buy property, as higher prices for commodity products  mean  less money to invest overall.  Increasing mortgage rates France used to have very low rates for mortgages before 2022. After the conflict, the mortgage rates went from 1% in beginning of 2022 to 3% in 2023, mostly because of the European Central   Bank policies . This affects the ability of people to buy apartments and houses, especially in the green real estate market where prices are higher at sale. Increasing buying power Despite increasing prices, the buying power of French citizens is steadily increasing  each quarter. This means that eventually, the French will be able to buy property once the mortgage rates get lower. Threat of substitution   Substitution of product by product: low In France, the substitution product by product level can be considered low. One specific property or apartment may be very different from another one as there are unique features of each including the location, layout, investment potential, any physical improvements e.g., renovations etc. Thus, all these features may limit the product-by-product substitution in this case.  Substitution of a need: low In this case, a need can be defined as a need for housing or an investment. People wish to use real estate in a number of ways: The need for housing This category entails buying property for living. In this case, this need may be substituted by renting, sharing accommodation or others. In all these cases real estate remains a fundamental aspect for fulfilling the need for housing.   The need for investment Real estate is a popular asset for investment. However, there may be alternatives of investment e.g., in shares, bonds and financial instruments which provide investors the possibility to fulfill their desire for investment.  The need to earn from property Property may also be used in commercial ways e.g., renting, leasing, providing it as a shared office space etc., providing customers the alternative through cost saving and with limited long-term responsibility.  In all these options, it may be seen that real estate remains an asset which can be used in a diverse range of ways and is not easily replaced or substituted by other solutions.  Substitution of product category: low The substitution of product category may be considered investment into alternative assets like financial instruments of bonds and shares. However, while such options are available, real estate is known for a more stable source of income, they have more growth potential in the long run, provide possibility for diversification and more. Thus, it is hardly possible to be fully substituted by another product category.  Overall, the threat of substitution can be defined as low. Industry life cycle: introduction /growth   As mentioned before, the green buildings market or the sustainable real estate market in France is in the introduction to growth stage. Homogeneity of products: low As only a few companies are offering sustainable real estate, the options for the customers are limited. Fixed costs: medium As real estate firms don’t produce anything, the only considerable fixed cost would be certain office expenses such as rent, utilities, maintenance etc. Moreover, the cost of purchasing technologies such as computers, printers and licensing for them, as well as software may add up to the fixed cost. Thus, it may be described as medium.  Variable costs: medium These include marketing and advertising expenses and vary depending on number of property listings and marketing strategies. Moreover, transaction expenses such as inspections, notary services, legal fees etc. would also depend on number of transactions and their complexity. Following the previously mentioned factors, we can say that there is a large number of competitors in each geographical market, where each individual brokerage company competes with others on the basis of services offered vs. the price of the commission. It is important to note that each broker is not highly different from another, and that when a customer decides to sell his property, the final choice of salesman is done in an individual and highly subjective matter. Therefore, we can conclude that the competitive rivalry between real estate agencies is high. Bargaining power of buyers Information accessible: the buyers have information they need  The real estate market and more precisely the brokerage market is quite different from others, as most real estate agencies offer the same service (sales) to various customers, by acting as mere middlemen between buyer and seller and getting a commission from each sale. Moreover, in France, the legal side of the sale is performed by a notary, thus reducing even more the need and specialization of each real estate broker. Hence, the customers (e.g., buyers and sellers of property) have a large variety of intermediaries to choose from and the choice is made on the basis of a few factors, such as physical proximity (e.g., a person selling an apartment located in Paris won’t be willing to put it on sale in Toulouse), commission amount (people want it to be as small as possible) and personal relationships with the broker himself.  Switching costs: low The cost for a customer is low as they can easily go to another real estate agency, explore the different options and work with different brokers. They can in this case consider more variety and range of properties, the possible locations, opportunities for investment and would have a more likelihood of finding the property they need. Thus, it can be said that the switching costs for customers are low, since customers have access to a large amount of information quite easily. By becoming one of the only sustainable real estate franchises in France, it can be possible to manage the power of buyers. Since KM’s offering will be certainly different from other companies, their bargaining power will be strongly lowered, making commission amounts higher (if KM wishes to do so), hence making the process more profitable overall.  All of these factors to the power of the buyers and sellers which is in this case high. Part 1.4 Competitive analysis Part 1.4.1 Kiinteistömaailma’s competitors on the French market Kiinteistömaailma will be competing with several other agencies depending on which city they decide to enter in.  As mentioned in the Porter’s 5 forces analysis, there are currently three types of agencies operating in France.  The first one is privately owned. As KM will not be competing on the traditional real estate market, these individual real estate firms are not really a threat to KM’s  operation  and are not direct competitors. However, it is still important to take these into account, as some of them are already specializing in sustainable real estate, in which case KM will be competing with them.  The second type of real estate firms and main competitors of KM are large real estate chains that also work on the basis of franchising  and cooperation .  These agencies are also usually individually owned, but use the brand name and sales portal of the firm by which they are owned. As the real estate industry is strongly reliant on trust and reputation, being part of a popular brand is important for the development of realtors. These large companies also have more financial power and have therefore more possibilities for marketing houses. The last type is  online real estate agencies. These have emerged only quite recently, but became attractive thanks to very competitively-priced fixed commissions. These could become a threat in the future, but they currently represent a very small  percentage of overall sales. Another potential competitor which could become  a partner in the future are sustainable real estate developers.  These firms focus on building projects of different scales  (tall towers, smaller eco-neighborhoods)  with sustainability at its core. They are currently competitors, because  most of them also have a sales division,  i.e.,  the sale of each sustainable building is done by themselves and not by other realty companies. Specialized small firms There are thousands of individually-owned real estate firms in France. While many of them sell essentially the same type of properties, some are more specialized than others. For instance,  firms like Arbre Immobilier  are fully specialized in the sale of sustainable housing.  To be more precise, they exclusively sell and rent sustainable houses and apartments in all of France. Beyond the sales process, they also focus on educating people on the importance of such housing and its impact on heath and the environment.  They also offer advice on retrofitting (making older houses more energy efficient and environmentally friendly)  for free.  It is important to differentiate specialized eco-friendly firms from traditional individual real estate agencies who seem to be sustainable, but are in reality simply greenwashing. For instance,  Matei  Immobilier , a firm based in Corsica, advertises the “eco-friendliness” of their operations. However, when looking at the properties they have on sale, you can see that they are not sustainable at all and don’t have any specialization in terms of sustainable  housing. This is a bad example of a sustainable real estate firm and KM should not follow their path. Another firm which focuses  on selling sustainable real estate is called Immobilier Ecologique .  Similarly  to Arbre Immobilier , they sell sustainable housing only. However, in contrast with the previous realtor, Immobilier Ecologique are more of a “housing portal”, where individuals can put their own houses on sale, at the condition that they are sustainable.  Both of these firms operate on a very small scale if compared to traditional real estate agencies. First of all, they are not attached to a particular region but focus on the whole country. Second, they have a small  number  of properties for sale (90 for Arbre Immobilier and  84 for Immobilier Ecologique) if compared to other real estate companies that sell traditional real estate in the whole country. For example, a seloger.com, which is France’s largest real estate housing portal (like  I mmobilier  E cologique)  currently offers almost 700.000 properties for sale. All in all, these agencies could become a small threat in the future (if the sustainable real estate market starts to develop) but due to the low number of properties they have on sale, they are not really a notable threat to KM’s expansion. Large real estate franchises  and cooperatives Since Kiinteistömaailma is a franchised company,  it is important to understand how they compare to local competition, even if real estate franchises focusing on sustainable buildings don’t exist yet.  These large companies are divided in two: franchised real estate firms and cooperatives. In the former, the decisions are taken by the firm and each franchisee must follow their rules, without having any saying in it. In the latter, each real estate agency that is part of a cooperative company has a voice and can influence the decisions taken by the company that owns them.  The largest and most profitable franchise in France is called Laforet ,  who has 720 franchises, 4800 agents all over France and around 18.029 properties for sale in May 2023. Voted the “most trusted real estate firm in 2022”, it is currently the most popular real estate franchise. Another franchise that originates from the USA, Century 2 1 , has 950 franchises in France and around 19.000 properties on sale.  The last notable competitor is Orpi , which is the largest real estate firm in France. As mentioned before, Orpi is one of few cooperatives. With 1350 agencies currently operating ,  8.000 realtors  and 31.000 properties on sale  it is definitely the most powerful actor in the real estate industry in France. The idea of a cooperative is that the owner of each Orpi agency has 1 voice that he can use to vote on the future of the business. These firms work on massive scales and competing directly with them would not be advisable for KM, which is why they should focus on sustainable housing only. These firms are at the same time a threat and a land of possibility, because if a large firm like Orpi starts to focus on selling sustainable housing, the market could change and there would be a surge in popularity in this type of building. However, if Orpi moves to  sustainable housing before KM manages to enter the French market, it would then mean that the entrance should be cancelled and the whole project nullified, as the first-mover advantage becomes less prominent. Online real estate agencies Several online agencies have existed for over 20 years, but their success has not yet been phenomenal, and only a limited amount of people use these services to sell their property. Moreover, there aren’t any online agencies that exclusively sell sustainable real estate. Some  notable online agencies are for instance Imop , who are currently the leaders of this industry. By offering a fixed commission of 4500eur, they manage to compete on cost leadership, while selling high-end housing.  All in all, these are not yet a threat as they also don’t yet specialize in sustainable real estate and are overall less threatening than more traditional real estate companies. Sustainable real estate developers The last but certainly not the least is real estate developers, more specifically ones that specialize in sustainable buildings. These firms are of prime importance for Kiinteistömaailma, and to ensure success in this market, KM must know how to manage them or how to work with them. Since 2009, the French government has launched the program called “EcoQuartier” (translated to eco-neighborhood) which encourages the development of sustainable cities, neighborhoods and buildings. The program aims at “promoting the emergence of a new way of designing, building and managing the city sustainably, by equipping, raising awareness and promoting project leaders ” . Thanks to this program, various actors of the real estate industry have started to shift towards  building sustainable housing. The first and most popular sustainable real estate developer is called Elithis , who have built their first large scale project back in 2009 in Dijon, called Tour Elithis (Elithis Tower) , which was built to accommodate offices . This project is very important for the sustainable buildings industry, as it is the first “energy-plus building” in the world, which means that it produces more energy coming from renewable sources than it imports from external sources,  i.e.,  it is  self-sustainable .  Elithis currently has 6 planned projects  in different cities such as Saint-Etienne, Dijon,  Mulhouse  and Le Havre.  Another tower that they have built is located in Strasbourg, which was completed in 2018. It is also record-breaking, as it is the first “energy-plus building” in the world in the residential sector. They achieved this prowess while maintaining standard production costs. Elithis also focus on retrofitting (upgrading older houses to be more sustainable) and collaborate with individual construction companies to i mprove  their efficiency .  Another  notable  competitor  who sells sustainable buildings and projects is Woodeum. The specificity of this firm is that they exclusively work with natural materials such as wood. They have been acquired at 100% by the Altarea group in 2023, which shows that even traditional real estate firms see the potential in sustainable real estate. All in all, these firms are very influential and having them as competitors would be extremely threatening. For this reason, it would be advisable for KM to try and collaborate with these firms, since they could benefit from KM’s know-how. Moreover, firms that only focus on the sale of sustainable office spaces aren’t really a threat, since residential and commercial real estate are not substitutes of each other. OPPORTUNITIES Government support Throughout the paper, the  existence  of support in the form of grants and  has been revealed. Since 2009, the French government has launched the project “EcoQuartier”, which includes various grants that  the government offers in collaboration with regions to create incentives for firms and individual entrepreneurs so that they build sustainable instead of traditional and retrofit their existing houses. On a larger level, the EU are also going to implement change s to encourage businesses to build green buildings. To be more specific, EU’s goal is to allow only green houses to be built by 2050 .  This is great news for KM, since they will be first-movers in this sector and therefore will have an advantage over other firms that will eventually have to move on to selling sustainable real estate as well. Development of technologies related to sustainable living leading to more affordable materials The current barrier to entry for sustainability overall is an increased price point in  comparison  with the non-sustainable counterpart. In his paper Drawdown, the author Paul Hawken talked about various new technologies that have emerged which can be used in sustainable real estate.  In the beginning of the life cycle of these products, they were very expensive to develop and were not economically beneficial to the end user. However, with the recent rises in energy prices and inflation , the usage of more energy efficient materials and technology has become financially viable, hence creating the need for sustainable buildings. Positive shift in social consciousness According to a study performed by the UK government in 2019 , people are now more conscient about environmental issues than ever before. This raise in concern is positive for Kiinteistömaailma, since their project is environment-friendly and helps reducing CO2 and GHG emissions overall Graph showing the evolution of the concern about the environment in the UK. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the interest rate was virtually 0 and people were able to afford new property. Now that mortgage rates have increased, people are no longer able to afford real estate. As discussed before, sustainable real estate is a bit more expensive in the initial phase, but the cost of usage is lower. This becomes a huge threat for KM because people can’t even afford normal real estate, so buying sustainable real estate is even less attractive. However, based on the interview (Appendix A), the real estate industry remains attractive for investors and once the interest rates will start going down, the real estate market will start to get active again. Hence, the scenario planning matrix will have to take it into account and make previsions on the long term, since these changes do take an extended period of time to take effect. Sustainable real estate is more expensive than traditional property Following the first threat about high interest rates,  the fact that sustainable real estate is inevitably more expensive than traditional real estate and its prices are not expected to drop (Appendix A), KM must learn to target a specific customer segment which is ready to pay the premium upfront and benefit from the lower costs in the long run. All in all, the SWOT matrix helps us understand the challenges and opportunities faced by the company and will allow us to create a more precise and adapted scenario planning matrix for the firm to enter the French market.  Part 2.2  A lternatives regarding mode of entry and strategical part of   sustainability   Part 2.2.1 Mode of entry After  analyzing  internal and external factors related to the company, including the market, competition and strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats according to key success factors in the industry, the mode of entry of the firm in the French market can be discussed and conclusions can be made. In order to support the choice of entry mode, the use of instruments for analysis is necessary. Therefore, to determine the mode of entry of Kiinteistömaailma into the French market in the sustainable buildings industry, the OLI Paradigm framework will be used.  Part 2.2.2 OLI Paradigm To start off, the OLI paradigm (also called eclectic paradigm) is a framework that helps companies determine their internationalization strategy by elimination. In essence, by looking at three advantages that the company has (ownership, location and internationalization advantages), it becomes possible to eliminate some forms of expansion strategy (acquisition, licensing, takeover…). Hence, the goal is to have a few options left at the end and choose the best one.  OLI Paradigm framework Ownership This factor discusses the advantage that the company has over some intangible resources and capabilities. As discussed previously, the company has an advantage in terms of knowledge in the sustainable buildings industry.  This knowledge is only developed through years of experience and is difficult to reproduce by most companies. As sustainability is a core value of Kiinteistömaailma, it is embedded in their organization which is not the case for most, or even all companies on the French market. Therefore, having this unique knowledge advantage over competitors means that KM indeed has the ownership advantage and hence does not have to remain domestic (according to the OLI Paradigm). Location   Since Kiinteistömaailma possesses the ownership advantage, we can now take a look at the location advantage, which  implies the idea of France being an advantageous location for Kiinteistömaailma’s development. Once again, this was discussed in the paper previously and we have come to the conclusion that the location is ideal for such an expansion. The local sustainable building market is strongly developing with actors that are committed to  creating  environmentally friendly and socially beneficial housing. Another advantage of the French market is that the climate is generally pretty good, which means that the  architecture  can be adapted to use more natural light and the use of solar panels is more effective than in other countries which are not as sunny ( e.g.,  Finland, Russia). Therefore, we can conclude that Kiinteistömaailma  will  possess the location advantage thanks to its 30  yearlong   know-hows  in the sustainability industry and the positive climate conditions in France that affect positively the development of sustainable real estate. This means that they won’t have to stay  at home and that expanding and going international is a good option.  Internationalization The last factor discusses whether it is more effective to do everything in-house or to rely on 3 rd  parties in the country we want to expand to. In the case of Kiinteistömaailma, this is where it might get complex . If the firm would decide to fully expand to France and not rely on the local workforce or specialists, it would mean that they would have to rely on their own knowledge and experience. However, it was discussed that one weakness of Kiinteistömaailma is their lack of experience in France. Moreover, fully expanding to France would incur a huge amount of costs  since the firm would have to buy agencies nationwide and train a huge  number  of staff to be on par with the standards of Kiinteistömaailma. Considering KM’s revenue was only 3.6M  eur  in 2022  and  interest rates are quite high, it seems that Kiinteistömaailma does not really possess the internationalization advantage, meaning that they cannot fully invest themselves into Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), which would mean a complete M&A operation. Hence, since the company does not have an internationalization advantage, they should focus on another type of market entry: franchising. In conclusion, we have found out that Kiinteistömaailma indeed have ownership and location advantages, but that the internationalization advantage is not as potent and therefore that the mode of entry must be different. According to the OLI paradigm, the choice of entry mode is therefore the one of franchising. In this case, the firm will be able to benefit from a strong footing  in the market thanks to access to local professionals but also retain some form of control and benefit from their own know-how.  Hence, the idea for the mode of entry would be through franchising existing individual real estate firms. Preferably, KM would start by contacting the sustainable real estate firms to offer them access to a large unified platform for selling sustainable real estate. These individual firms would also benefit from training courses aimed at improving their marketing capabilities of selling sustainable real estate, since KM has knowledge in selling them.   This way, the firms get access to a better way at selling their properties, access to KM’s know-how and a sales portal. On the other hand, Kiinteistömaailma gets a footing in the  French  market without having to acquire agencies and having to spend large amounts of money.  Part 2.3 Deloitte Scenario Planning Part 2.3.1  Scenario planning  introduction Now that the firm and its internal and external  metrics were analyzed and the mode of entry was determined,  it is now time to adapt the future strategy of the company according to different possible scenarios. In a fast-paced world, changes in strategy must be done quickly which is why companies prepare their future plans in advance. The Deloitte Scenario planning matrix (2x2) is a powerful framework that can help companies determine and adapt their strategy.  2x2 Scenario planning matrix Each scenario relies on the development of 2 so called “drivers”, which are in essence some selective external factors. This is measured  by 2 extremes – minimum and maximum development (see Fig. 20.). Then, 4 scenarios are created according to the situation. For example, in Scenario 4, the Driver 1 is minimally developed, while Driver 2 is maximally developed. The same logic applies to all 4 scenarios. Hence, once the manager of the company gets to see these predictable scenarios, he will be able to make the best decision according to the development of drivers. It is important to note that this framework is not adapter to unpredictable future  occurrences . For instance, a scenario planning matrix created in 2019 could not have predicted the huge impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the business world.  Moreover, each scenario holds a list of various factors  that increase or decrease according to the development of previously mentioned drivers.  The factors range from “Down” to “Up”. For instance, Deloitte used the following factors on their paper about the pandemic, titled “Building resilience amid volatility” .  Example of several factors that characterize a scenario Now that we have described the components of the scenario planning matrix, we can start building one for Kiinteistömaailma specifically, starting with the description of drivers. Part 2.3.2  Drivers’  description As mentioned before, drivers are external factors that are expected to develop. However, it is impossible to predict how exactly the future is shaped. Therefore, each scenario will be adapted according to the development of these factors.  Based on the research done throughout the paper, two drivers were selected to be used in the scenario planning. The first one is the development of sustainable real estate, which has 2 possible dimensions: more mainstream and less mainstream. The second driver that was selected is energy price level, which can be similar/increased (because it is currently high) or decreased  emery  price. Since the development of housing usually takes many years to change, the perspective of the scenario planning is chosen to be within 10 years.  One reason for the choice of timeframe is that the market needs time to adapt and competitors might start to get active in the sustainable real estate market once they see its benefits.  Driver 1: development of sustainable real estate  (more/less mainstream) The first driver and uncertainty that will have to be tracked is the development of sustainable real estate.  Although there  is  currently no metric to track the number of traditional vs. sustainable buildings in France, an estimate would be about less than 5%, because the part of sustainable buildings in Finland is about 10% (Appendix A) and Finland is more advanced in terms of sustainability than France. As soon as these metrics become available, they should be put into use by Kiinteistömaailma. Looking at the  10-year  time frame, it could be considered that this driver would be high if sustainable buildings will represent more than 15% of the total housing “pool”. Hence, anything >15% would  mean that sustainable real has become more mainstream. On the other hand, if in 10 years, less than 10% of the housing pool is composed of sustainable real estate, it would mean that sustainable real estate failed to become mainstream and therefore the scenario will also be different. In turn, this means that the strategy has to be adapted.  Another way of measuring the  development of this driver is through nation-wide surveys. For instance, respondents could be asked yearly about their interest and preoccupation about living sustainably and also about living in a sustainable building. For example, the graph below shows the importance attached to environmental issues by the  French  population in 2019. What importance do you attach to environmental issues? Source: Statista Once sustainability becomes a truly concerning matter for the French population and they start understanding that the way to a  zero-carbon  future and a low GHG emissions future is only possible if people start upgrading their own homes.  The trigger for this driver would be when an increasing number of people start inquiring various firms about acquiring sustainable housing and refusing to buy any other type of property. Driver 2: energy price level  (increase, stay as high / decrease) The second driver is about the price of energy.  One important notion that is important to grasp about housing is that most of housing in large cities was built before the 1970’s, when energy prices were irrelevant because their price was so low. After the 1970 oil crisis, people started shifting from the use of gas to power their houses to electricity, since its price was also quite low. However, as recent events have caused havoc in the energy industry, the price of electricity has almost tripled between 2019-2023 , going from 0.08eur/kWh in 2018 to 0.20eur/kWh in 2023.  Now that electricity prices are so high, people must find alternatives to power their houses in a relatively cheaper way.  Hence, the higher the price for energy, the more useful the sustainable housing becomes. This driver is even more straightforward to measure than for interest in sustainability. Eurostat published average energy prices in EU every quarter, so Kiinteistömaailma can follow the trends and adapt their strategy accordingly.  The so-called trigger for this driver would be if prices remain high for an extended period of time (1 year) or relatively low. Energy prices in EU from 2018-2023. Source: Eurostat Popularity of sustainable real estate vs. energy prices on the scenario planning matrix Part 2.3.3  Factors description Now that we have a good understanding of the drivers of change and its assumptions, we can move to analyzing the development of each factor  according to the four different scenarios. Each factor has its own attribute and can be Low, Moderate and High. Factor 1: interest for sustainable housing The first factor looks at the interest allocated to sustainable housing on a more individual level. As the  level of knowledge about sustainability increases, so should the interest towards the subject. Although interest for sustainability is at an  all-time  high right now, the interest for sustainable buildings has not yet peaked. Therefore, this factor is currently set to low, but is expected to raise in future years. The ideal scenario would be to have this factor at High. Factor 2: Sustainable housing prices The second factor is directly related to the economy. In today’s world, people are forces to pay a “sustainability surplus” for everything, meaning that sustainable houses are more expensive than traditional property due to a higher level of technology and higher cost of materials. Another reason why sustainable  houses are more expensive is due to very low supply. Even if demand is quite low, the supply is even lower, causing a price surplus. In an ideal world, sustainable housing would become even cheaper than traditional real estate. However, this is quite unlikely so even matching the price of traditional real estate would be a positive outcome. Factor 3: Competition level  As the industry and demand will start growing, so will the competition. However, if the sustainable industry proves to be inefficient, then competition will remain low. As time goes by, market concentration will start to get lower because new players will enter the industry.  Factor 4: Government support It is in the government’s needs to encourage sustainable building because  it is a great investment opportunity and a great way to reach environmental goals. Help in the form of financial grants will also encourage foreign investors to join the  French  market and start developing various projects.  Once the industry starts to develop on a faster pace, the government can start reducing the amount of support, which is not necessarily bad. Factor 5: Number of sustainable housing available Finally, the last factor is purely numerical. As the energy prices will start growing and the demand for sustainable housing will rise, the number of available sustainable buildings will also start increasing.  Part 2.3.4  Creation of the scenario planning matrix Now that we have explained the drivers of the scenario matrix and each of its 5 factors, we can now construct the scenario planning matrix for  Kiinteistömaailma’s  expansion towards the  French  market in the sustainable real estate industry.  2x2 Scenario Planning matrix for Kiinteistömaailma’s entry of the French sustainable real estate market Each scenario is based on the increase or decrease of both drivers and is defined by several factors that can be Low,  Moderate  or High. Adapt strategy This first scenario is defined by high development of sustainable real estate, meaning that a lot of sustainable buildings are being constructed, but at the same time the energy prices start going down and electricity is therefore affordable. In this scenario, the interest for sustainable housing is high, because if firms are building an increasing  number  of sustainable  houses,  it means that there has been in increased level of interest and demand from consumers. Housing prices become moderate, because thanks to high government support and economies of scale, firms are able to reduce costs. Competition level is also moderate, because firms will start seeing the potential of sustainable real estate but will not risk going all-in because the energy prices will  be low enough to keep traditional real estate attractive from a financial standpoint. Government support will be moderate from this point onwards because the low energy prices will make it hard to convince people to buy such real estate. Finally, the number of sustainable housing will be moderate since once again, the industry is highly developed but until the largest real estate firms are on board, developers will be hesitant to building a huge  number  of properties if they are not sure that demand will be sufficient. If this scenario would become reality, then the recommendation would be to  adapt the firm’s strategy and marketing, accentuating on the health benefits of sustainable housing instead of talking about reduced costs. The benefit of this scenario is that KM will have a large offering panel,  i.e.,  that they will have a lot of products to sell. In such case, proposing lower commissions than their competitors from traditional real estate could be a solution . All  in all, this scenario is far from ideal, but at least the industry they work in is developing quite well.    Sustainable future The second scenario is the best possible alternative  out of all of them. In this case, energy prices are high which means that sustainable buildings become more financially viable than traditional real estate and popularity of sustainable real estate becomes high in consequence.  It means that interest for sustainable real estate will be high (because it will become a need), prices will be moderate due to high supply and relatively high demand, competition level will be very high since all of the large realtor firms will jump on the occasion of selling sustainable real estate. Government support will be moderate/low since the industry will be developing quickly and due to economies of scale created by companies, grants will not be as useful as before. Finally, the number of sustainable housing will of course be high.  Strategically, this means that KM will have the first-mover advantage and will be ahead of all competition thanks to their accumulated 30 years of experience in the industry and the 10 years of experience on the French market. This means that they will be able to expand to many new cities where sustainable buildings will be thriving. Their brand image will be associated with sustainability which could make them the largest firm on the sustainable real estate market.  Kiinteistömaailma could become the Tesla of the sustainable real estate industry.  In this scenario, the future becomes sustainable and major progress is being done.  This is the perfect scenario for Kiinteistömaailma.  Missed opportunity In this scenario, all drivers are at their lowest, meaning that energy prices reach the lowest point and sustainable real estate is no longer developing. Prices remain high, government support, although still existing, starts to go down because they will realize that the industry is dying and all other factors are low.  The recommendation for Kiinteistömaailma would therefore be to leave the country and learn from mistakes, as there is no future for them in France. Exit costs are relatively low in comparison with other industries, so it is not a huge issue for KM. The firm should just view this as a missed opportunity and start focusing on working in their home market, in Finland. Exclusive partnerships The last scenario is the one where energy prices keep growing, but sustainable real estate fails to democratize and remains unpopular. Regarding factors, the interest in sustainability is moderate, since people will inevitable start thinking about reducing their energy consumption to reduce costs, but not enough to get fully involved. Prices remain high  and competition is low because there is close to no demand. On the other hand, government support is very high because reducing energy costs will become a priority for the government. The number of sustainable buildings will be moderate as firms will start seeing these high prices as an opportunity for success. From a strategic point of view, it would be a good idea for Kiinteistömaailma to start partnering up with various firms in order to increase the marketing of sustainable buildings and in some sense push the popularity of sustainable real estate up. By trying to bargain on prices with developers and other stakeholders, Kiinteistömaailma could potentially save the market and in the process grow their own sources of income. CONCLUSION After completing all the necessary analysis and cre a ting a set of recommendation , we can finally conclude the paper. First of all, we should recall our initial objectives of this paper: analyze the company to understand its structure define the sustainability strategy according to resources and capabilities while taking into account external and internal environment.  evaluate the French market for the potential expansion review expansion strategies in the French market create a scenario planning matrix to formulate options for KM’s expansion depending on changes in trends and markets Create recommendations for the company according to different scenarios In the beginning of the paper, Kiinteistömaailma was analyzed and described as a leading real estate franchise company founded in 1990 and based in Finland  which operates in the  Baltics  region . With  100 individual agencies  and over 700 agents in the whole country, it  is  the largest real estate company in Finland and has around 20% market share, with their closest competitor having 13% (RE/MAX).  Our goal was to develop a successful strategy for the successful expansion of the firm towards the French market  in the sustainable buildings industry . This market was chosen according to the  internal wish of the company’s management. After defining the company, we started to describe the real estate industry globally before talking more precisely about sustainable real estate, which is a new upcoming market that has huge potential. Considering that around 40% of all CO2 emissions are caused by buildings, it became clear that an industry that helps the environment is the future. Through this analysis, we discovered that sustainable real estate exists thanks to various technologies, which were described by Paul Hawken in his book Drawdown. Each of these technologies helps reducing environmental harm in their own way. However, it was also discovered that sustainable buildings are not only advantageous on the environmental side of things, but also have social and financial benefits which are all part of the Triple Bottom Line theory. For instance, installing new heating systems and isolation helps to retain heat for longer in houses and can help reducing the energy costs.  Installing green roofs in new buildings helped to improve social interactions and health of its inhabitants. Finally, replacing traditional lighting with LED lamps helps to reduce the use of  fossil  energy and therefore to reduce the environmental impact of buildings. After getting a clear view on the benefits of sustainable housing, we started to review the French market and its peculiarities. To start off, we used the PESTEL analysis to  get a better grasp at the external factors influencing the French market. There were both positive (political stability,  increasing  buying power, technological advancements) and negative factors (increasing mortgage rates, unemployment). Moreover, we noted that the green buildings industry was in its introduction/growth phase, which means that KM will be able to benefit from the first-mover advantage when entering the market.  Then we moved on to Porter’s 5 forces analysis, where we concluded on the various powers (suppliers, buyers/sellers), threats (new entrants, substitution) and finally concluded that the competitive rivalry is high on the French market. From the competitive analysis, we understood the competitive field in France. To be more precise, there are different types of direct and indirect competitors. The largest constituent of the market are individual realtors (65% of the market), followed by large real estate chains and cooperatives (25%) and finally online agencies (10%). It is important to know that while real estate chains only made up 25% of the whole competitive pool, they created more than 40% of all the revenue. The sustainable real estate building companies were also included, since they also sold their own properties to customers. If KM does not manage to collaborate with these firms, developing on the market might get complicated as they are a huge threat to the firm.  After  analyzing  the competitors, we moved to determining the Key Success Factors in the industry and found them to be the following:  For franchises and individual realtors Precise price determination (not too high since it won’t be sold, not too low since customer will change realtors) Well determined commission price Good brand image For sustainable real estate building companies Offer financial advantages to customers in the long run (e.g., by buying sustainable, customers will benefit from cheaper energy bills thanks to more efficient use of energy in buildings) Differentiation from traditional real estate   Thanks to those KSF, we were able to determine the primary SWOT matrix of Kiinteistömaailma. Some notable strengths were the first-mover advantage, high know-how in the field. KM also has some weaknesses, such as the lack of experience on the French market and no brand image. On the other hand, the firm also has opportunities from external factors, such as government support or development of new technologies. Finally, some of the threats were the high interest rates for mortgages and the so-called “sustainability premium”, meaning that anything sustainable is more expensive than its traditional, non-sustainable counterpart. After reviewin g  the company, the market and its opportunities, we moved on t he main part of the paper which is the expansion of the company. The  first  step was to determine the mode of entry of the firm into the sustainable real estate market in France. By using the OLI Paradigm, we concluded that the firm is not yet  capable  of fully engaging into Foreign Direct Investment (FDI), but can definitely start franchising individual real estate firms and exclusively sell sustainable real estate through them. As franchisees, the advantages that they get are numerous. First of all, they will receive training from Kiinteistömaailma which possesses the know-how in selling sustainable real estate. Then, they will get access to a common sales portal which will be focused on selling sustainable real estate only. Finally, once the firm will be on the market for an extended period of  time,  they will benefit from the positive brand image associated with Kiinteistömaailma. On the other hand, Kiinteistömaailma will have huge amount of costs associated with buying agencies and hiring employees, as this will already be available at the individual firms. Finally, after knowing that the company will enter the market through franchising, it was time to give the company specific instructions according to various scenarios. Hence, the 2x2 scenario planning matrix was created using the two following drivers (from low to high): development of sustainable real estate vs energy prices. Then, the following 5 factors were used (ranging from Low, Moderate to High): interest for sustainable housing, sustainable housing prices, competition level, government support, number of sustainable housing. By combining these factors and the drivers, we obtained the following matrix, with 4 scenarios based on uncertainties. For each scenario, a different strategy was proposed, with the best scenario being “sustainable future”, and the least desirable one being “missed opportunity”. With this set of recommendations, Kiinteistömaailma is now ready to enter the French market and discover new opportunities on foreign markets. Thanks to this new innovative market, Kiinteistömaailma can not only benefit themselves, but also to improve the lives of millions of people in France and hopefully in Europe. As discussed in the paper, sustainability is not only a matter of saving the planet, but also the one of improving people’s health in general through the use of new technologies that filter toxic air particles or use new, healthier lighting options. To conclude, Kiinteistömaailma is ready to adapt to various scenarios that may appear in the future thanks to a deep analysis of uncertainties. The interview took place on Tuesday, May 23, 2023 in the Töölö office. The firm How long have you been working in real estate? About 10 years (main job), worked in the industry for 30 years How interested is Kiinteistömaailma in selling responsible and environmentally friendly properties in the future? A lot. In addition, Kiinteistömaailma is interested in the sustainability of its own operations and is the first brokerage chain to publish its own sustainability Report: https://kiinteistomaailma.sharepoint.com/sites/ajankohtaista/SitePages/3.4.-Vastuullisuusraportti-2022.aspx Has the acquisition of Ober- haus  in 2021 had a positive impact on  Real Estate World's operations in Finland?  There is unfortunately no information Why would the French market be a good way to expand the company?  If Kiinteistömaailma wants to expand further internationally, France, as well as Spain and Italy, are probably the logical next areas (after Baltic countries).  Sustainability in real estate When did the terms "sustainability", "eco-friendly" and "low-energy house" start appearing in the real estate industry?  This has become more important in the 2000s, when awareness increased and, on the other hand, energy costs increased. Do you believe that energy-efficient and sustainable buildings can replace traditional houses in the future?  In Finland, construction is strictly regulated, and energy efficiency is an important part of building regulations. Thus, in practice, energy-efficient and sustainable solutions replace traditional houses, because no other kind of construction will be allowed in the future. How many of the properties currently for sale can be considered sustainable and environmentally friendly (approximately)?  The best estimate is probably about 10% of all that is currently for sale in Finland, because most of them are old second-hand apartments and real estate, which are not equally affected by the current regulation. The old building stock will also be renewed little by little, and their energy efficiency will also be improved in connection with renovations. The regulation of this comes from the EU. Do you expect low energy home prices to fall in the future? No, I don’t  The Market How difficult would it be to exploit Real Estate know-how in a foreign market like France? Certainly not particularly difficult, but it would be important to know the local legislation on the subject, the so-called 'local legislation'. arbitration law. In addition, the “housing company” concept is a Finnish invention and, in that sense, the system in France is very different in this respect. In the rest of Europe, the system is also very conservative in other respects, when notaries etc. are needed. more in line with Finnish real estate transactions. Although prices have fallen slightly, is the real estate industry still attractive to investors? Absolutely it is. The housing market is significantly more stable than the normal stock market and, for many, a more concrete way to invest.  What size and type of real estate has become the most popular in recent years (houses, small apartments, large apartments)?  During the past year (after the coronavirus), the most popular apartments have been reasonably priced family apartments (3-4h) in a good location close to services. How attractive are foreign investments and acquisitions for a company like Real Estate World? I believe that the board of directors of Kiinteistömaailma are looking into these, but the industry is highly regulated and therefore international expansion is a multigenerational issue.
